nativeRange.habitat: Inhabits coral and rubble areas of deeper lagoon and seaward reefs, typically reported at depths of about 10–50 m (recorded range 0–60 m); generally observed singly, grazing surface algal films and detritus from hard substrate

decorPreferences: live rock with biofilm and algal turf for grazing
rockwork crevices and overhangs for shelter
long horizontal footprint preferred over tall depth
sand bed for natural grazing behaviour

diet.notes: Uses individually movable bristle-like teeth to sift detritus and graze surface algal films from live rock. Marine-based seaweed (nori) and spirulina-rich herbivore preparations are generally advised at least three times per week. Has been reported to accept algae-based flakes/pellets, frozen vegetable preparations, and small meaty items (mysis, krill) in mixed-community feedings; vitamin-supplemented diets are commonly recommended to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion.

goodWith: non-Ctenochaetus tangs (with caution and ample space)
avoidWith: similarly shaped or similarly coloured surgeonfish

summary: An orange-brown Indo-Pacific bristletooth surgeonfish with blue facial markings and two dark spots at the dorsal and anal-fin bases. Generally considered an efficient, reef-safe algae and detritus grazer for moderately sized reef systems.
careNotes:
The Two Spot Bristletooth Tang (Ctenochaetus binotatus) is a medium-sized Indo-Pacific surgeonfish named for the pair of dark spots at the base of its dorsal and anal fins. Adult body length is generally reported around 18–22 cm; FishBase records a maximum of 22.0 cm TL, while retail sources commonly state about 8 inches (~20 cm).

A tank around 400 L (~105 gal) with a long horizontal footprint is generally advised. Retail sources span 70 gal (265 L, LiveAquaria) to 90 gal (340 L, Aquatics Unlimited) to about 132 gal (500 L, ReefApp). The cautious-larger choice reflects that bristletooth tangs are active grazers throughout the day, can show territorial behaviour toward similar species, and benefit from open swimming space combined with extensive live rock for natural grazing routes.

Water parameters should be stable and well-oxygenated within standard reef ranges (SG ~1.022–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12). Temperature is commonly recommended around 24–27°C; LiveAquaria specifies 72–78°F (22–26°C) and FishBase's modelled preferred range is ~24.5–28.9°C with a mean near 27.7°C — averaged to a practical 24–27°C aquarium target.

Diet should lean heavily on marine algae. Dried seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based foods at least three times per week are generally advised; vitamin-rich diets are commonly suggested to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), a condition this species (like most Acanthurids) is reported to develop in poor water quality or on a vitamin-deficient diet. Has been documented grazing toxin-producing dinoflagellates from substrate films in the wild, which is the basis of historical ciguatera reports for this species (relevant for handlers/edibility, not for aquarium safety).

The species is generally considered reef-safe; reports of coral nipping are uncommon and tend to involve under-fed individuals. It is best kept singly per tank — conspecific aggression toward other Ctenochaetus and similarly shaped tangs has been reported as high — and is generally peaceable with non-tang reef fish.

As an acanthurid, this species possesses sharp caudal-peduncle blades (the 'scalpel') used for defence. They are not venomous but can inflict lacerations on handlers and tankmates during conflicts; handle with care during transfers.

Cross-source notes: Adult max size ranged from 20 cm (retail '~8 in') to 22 cm TL (FishBase, ReefApp). Tank minimum spread 70–132 gal across sources; per the cautious-larger rule rounded to 400 L reflecting the species' active grazing behaviour and semi-territorial nature. Temperature spread 22–29°C across sources; averaged to 24–27°C. Care level was split (LiveAquaria 'moderate', Aquatics Unlimited 'easy') — recorded as intermediate per the ordinal-average rule. Native-range note: Aquatics Unlimited stated 'Indo-Pacific to Australia and Hawaii', but FishBase explicitly notes the species is absent from Hawaii; Hawaii was therefore not included in the countries list.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in available hobby sources. Like most acanthurids, Ctenochaetus binotatus is presumed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild, with planktonic larvae; spawning in home aquaria is considered highly unlikely given tank-size and pair-bonding constraints.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for Two Spot Bristletooth Tang. Provided max size (8 in / ~20 cm), tank minimum (70 gal / 265 L), temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), diet (herbivore, seaweed 3x/week), temperament (semi-aggressive toward other tangs, peaceful otherwise), reef compatibility (yes), and family (Acanthuridae). Reached via the tangs-surgeons collection root supplied in the brief.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked species identity (Ctenochaetus binotatus Randall, 1955), family (Acanthuridae) and order (Acanthuriformes), max size (22.0 cm TL), depth range (0–60 m, typical 10–50 m), preferred temperature (24.5–28.9°C, mean 27.7°C), Indo-Pacific native range from East Africa to Tuamotu Islands (explicitly absent from Red Sea, Gulf of Oman, Hawaii), habitat (coral and rubble areas of deep lagoon and seaward reefs), IUCN status (Least Concern, 2010), and ecology (usually solitary, grazes surface algae and detritus, has been documented consuming toxin-producing dinoflagellates).

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Hobby retailer secondary (Reef2Reef wiki had no species-specific page for C. binotatus). Confirmed max size (~8 in / 20 cm), minimum tank (90 gal / 340 L, larger than LiveAquaria's 70 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), semi-aggressive/territorial toward genus, reef-safe, and Indo-Pacific range. Stated 'to Hawaii' which conflicts with FishBase — Hawaii excluded from countries list.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Third hobby source consulted for tank-size triangulation. Stated max size 22 cm (8.7 in), minimum 500 L (~132 gal), temperature ~26°C, SG ~1.026, primarily algae/seaweed/spirulina diet, mostly peaceful but may show aggression toward similar species, always reef-safe.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records found in available hobby sources. Like most acanthurids, presumed pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild; spawning in aquaria is considered highly unlikely.
